patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 44-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset; further management may be discussed but should not require additional WRITE tools.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset' and explains what it means (distal lower-extremity weakness beginning in the anterior tibialis/foot dorsiflexors).

Meets the communication milestone by discussing the role of genetic testing (confirmatory testing, implications for prognosis and family counseling) and offers referral/coordination for testing without necessarily placing a referral order (since L1 tool sequence is diagnosis-only).
Elicits key history of progressive foot drop/ankle dorsiflexion weakness, tripping, and time course; asks about symmetry and functional impact (stairs, gait, falls).

Screens for red flags that would prompt urgent evaluation (rapid progression, bulbar symptoms such as dysphagia/dysarthria, respiratory symptoms, new severe proximal weakness, significant sensory loss, bowel/bladder dysfunction).
Asks about family history of similar weakness, gait problems, or known neuromuscular diagnoses; discusses inheritance possibility in plain language.

Performs or describes an appropriate focused neuromuscular exam: distal strength (ankle dorsiflexion), gait (steppage), muscle atrophy, reflexes, and sensory testing to help distinguish myopathy from neuropathy.

Provides an appropriate differential that includes at least: peroneal neuropathy, L5 radiculopathy, Charcot-Marie-Tooth/other hereditary neuropathies, inclusion body myositis, and motor neuron disease; explains why distal myopathy remains most consistent if sensory is preserved and pattern is anterior tibial onset.
Avoids inappropriate medication prescribing as definitive treatment for the myopathy; emphasizes supportive care (PT/OT, ankle-foot orthosis) and monitoring as discussion only.

If any medication discussion occurs, completes required safety checks in conversation before prescribing: asks about allergies before prescribing (critical); also considers drug interactions before prescribing (noncritical) and contraindications before ordering tests/therapies (noncritical).
Communicates uncertainty appropriately: frames diagnosis as likely/working if confirmatory testing (genetic testing and/or EMG/CK per local practice) has not yet been completed.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset and counsel about confirmatory genetic testing; no additional WRITE actions.
steps: Elicit pattern of weakness (distal, anterior tibial onset) and progression; assess functional impact and falls.
Screen for sensory symptoms and pain to distinguish myopathy vs neuropathy/radiculopathy.
Ask about family history and age at onset; review relevant exposures/medications that could mimic myopathy if applicable.
Perform/describe focused neuro exam: ankle dorsiflexion strength, gait (steppage), distal atrophy, reflexes, sensory exam.
Explain diagnosis in patient-friendly terms and explicitly mention genetic testing as the key confirmatory step; discuss implications for family.
Record diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Working diagnosis with explicit uncertainty pending confirmatory testing; still records the diagnosis as the leading diagnosis and emphasizes genetic testing.
steps: Gather core history/exam consistent with anterior tibial onset distal myopathy and rule out major red flags.
Explain it is the most likely diagnosis and that genetic testing is recommended to confirm and guide counseling.
Record diagnosis as distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset (leading/working).

acceptable_variants.description: Broader differential documented verbally before committing to the final diagnosis; still meets milestone by explaining diagnosis and genetic testing.
steps: Elicit symptoms and exam findings; discuss differential including peroneal neuropathy, L5 radiculopathy, hereditary neuropathy, inclusion body myositis.
Explain why distal myopathy with anterior tibial onset best fits the pattern (distal weakness, preserved sensation).
Discuss genetic testing as confirmatory and for family implications.
Record diagnosis.

patient.instructions:

Start by saying you’re having really bad pain all over like a sickle cell pain crisis and it’s not getting better. Volunteer: deep aching lower back/hip pain since yesterday worsening; chest/rib pain with deep breaths; you’ve been drinking water but can’t catch up; very tired.

If asked, report: pain 8–9/10, home meds not helping; low-grade fever and chills; new dry cough; short of breath walking to the bathroom; darker urine and peeing less; nausea and poor appetite.

Do not mention unless directly asked about meds/adherence, substance use, travel, or missed appointments: you ran out of daily sickle medicine 2 weeks ago; missed clinics due to no ride; long car trip 2 days ago with little water; mild yellow eyes; left upper belly fullness/soreness; new headaches for a week; alcohol most nights; sometimes extra pain pills from a friend.
